1: <header>Environmental burdens of small-scale intensive pig production in China </header>
Xin Liu, Zican Cai, Zengwei Yuan * State Key Laboratory of Pollution Control and Resource Reuse, School of the Environment, Nanjing University, Nanjing 210023, China
2:
<list>The Chinese system for small-scale in- tensive pig production is analyzed using LCA. 
Feed production is the principal contrib- utor to all the environmental impacts. 
Feed formula changes indicate higher contribution from the grower-finisher stage. 
Developing countries show poor opera- tion regarding greenhouse gas emis- sions. 
Feasible mitigation strategies are pro- posed for more sustainable pig production.</list>
4: Article history: Received 6 October 2020 Received in revised form 18 December 2020 Accepted 25 December 2020 Available online 21 January 2021
Keywords: Pig supply chain Life cycle assessment Feed Manure management Greenhouse gas emissions
5: Small-scale intensive pig production systems account for over a half of the total number of pig farms in China, of which concerns have been raised relating to their environmental performances. This study explores the cradle- to-slaughterhouse gate environmental impacts using life cycle assessment (LCA) approach, with the purpose of identifying major hotspots to formulate mitigation strategies. The functional unit is defined as 1000 kg of pig carcass weight. Consistent with previous research, feed production makes up the largest contribution (56-95%) to all the six selected impact categories based on the ReCiPe 2016 framework. Of the feed ingredients, maize is identified as the principal hotspot mainly due to the large consumption as well as the heat usage in grain steaming to enhance availability of starch digestion. The results also indicate that changes of feed consumption and composition along the lifetime growth reveal a much higher contribution from the grower-finisher stage. Marked differences are observed in terms of greenhouse gas emissions from pig production between developing (including China) and developed countries. With lots of studies showing feasibilities, improvements suggested for small-scale intensive pig production systems include the optimization of feed formulas, the introduction of new feed technologies, and the upgrade of manure management system. Our results provide valuable and prac- tical insight for the Chinese pig supply chain to mitigate environmental burdens and achieve future environmen- tal sustainability.

7: Global diets are becoming richer in animal-sourced foods, which provide high-quality protein and important micro-nutrients to address

undernourishment. It appears, though, that excess intake of animal- sourced foods may not only cause health problems but also threaten en- vironmental sustainability, e.g. greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions (Clark et al., 2019; Tilman and Clark, 2014; Willett et al., 2019). Similar to the global trends, Chinese diet also transfers towards an animal-based one and now leads to increased burdens on the environment and nutrition quality (He et al., 2018; Xiong et al., 2020). However, the diet shift in

China shows a heavy portion of pork other than poultry at the global scale. According to Food and Agriculture Organization (FAO), more than 60% of meat consumption in China in 2017 is pork, while at global scale (excluding China), 41% of meat consumption comes from poultry. To meet the huge demand of pork, China's pig production is growing at an unprecedented rate, accounting for approximately 45% of the global pig production in 2018 (FAO, 2020).

To comprehensively quantify the environmental performance, life cycle assessment (LCA) studies have been conducted on several pig pro- duction systems across the world. In general, these studies focus sepa- rately on three primary activities in pig production: feed production, whole-system pig production, and manure management (McAuliffe et al., 2016). Feed production generates more globalized environmental burdens while manure management is more problematic in localized burdens. LCAs on feed production have evaluated the environmental consequences of optimizing pig feed formula (Garcia-Launay et al., 2014; Monteiro et al., 2017; van Zanten et al., 2018) and precision feed- ing strategies (Andretta et al., 2018; Monteiro et al., 2016). LCA has also been applied considerably to pig slurry to analyze its environmental burdens using different treatment technologies, such as anaerobic di- gestion and slurry acidification (Pexas et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2017; Yuan et al., 2018). Most LCAs on whole-system pig production include both feed production and manure management but tend to have different system boundaries, e.g. cradle-to-farm gate or cradle-to- slaughterhouse gate (Cherubini et al., 2015; Lamnatou et al., 2016). De- spite that feed production has been mostly identified as the largest con- tributor to the environmental impacts, the influence, however, caused by feed formulas targeted for different stages of a pig's life has not been analyzed in detail, making it difficult to identify the hotspots of pig diets.

The environmental impacts of pig production have been investi- gated extensively in European countries (Dolman et al., 2012; Halberg et al., 2010; Lamnatou et al., 2016; Reckmann et al., 2013), the United States of America, Australia and Brazil (Cherubini et al., 2015; Pelletier et al., 2010; Wiedemann et al., 2016). In China, there are more LCA stud- ies on manure management than on the whole pig production chain. Wang et al. (2015) once assessed the environmental performance of a large-scale pig farm with an annual production of 100,000 finisher pigs in northern China. However, though China has stepped up policy support to promote standardized and scale farming, the large-scale

pig farms only account for about 2-3% of the total number of pig farms in China, while the majority still have the size of around 500 pigs to be slaughtered annually (so-called small-scale) (MARA, 2018). These smaller pig farms generally have limited knowledge and invest- ment capacity of frontier techniques to improve their environmental performances.

Therefore, in accordance with the ISO standards 14040 and 14044 (ISO 14040, 2006; ISO 14044, 2006), a cradle-to-slaughterhouse gate life cycle analysis is performed using the open-source software openLCA (version 1.9.0) to evaluate the environmental impacts, including global warming, terrestrial acidification, freshwater eutrophication potential and toxicity indicators, of small-scale intensive pig production systems in China. The contribution of feed formulas from different growth stages is also analyzed. This study enlightens the environmental hotspots of small-scale pig production systems in China and provides a benchmark for subsequent environmental improvements to move towards a sus- tainable agriculture industry.

11: The system boundary, as presented in Fig. 1, starts from off-site crop cultivation, feed processing, to on-site pig production. The functional unit (FU) is defined to be 1000 kg of pig carcass weight (CW) after the process in slaughterhouse.

On-site pig operations cover a pig's whole life cycle, including piglet production, pig rearing, manure management, to the final slaughtering. A pig's life cycle (see Fig. S1) starts from adult female pigs (gilts or sows) reach maturity and mate with adult male pigs (boars). The main differ- ence between gilts and SOWS lies in that whether they have given birth to their first litter before. After mating, adult female pigs have a preg- nancy (gestation) period of about 114 days. They are moved to a farrowing barn when ready to give birth (farrow). Usually, a female pig will fallow 8-12 baby pigs (piglets) per litter. The goal of most farmers is to have, on average, greater than two litters per sow per year. After giving birth, female pigs remain with their piglets, nursing for 3-4 weeks until the piglets are weaned (lactation). Then, they are ei- ther returned for mating or sent away for slaughter when they fail to rebreed.

Fig. 1. System boundary of small-scale intensive pig production in China. The blue arrows represent the life cycle of pig production; the brown arrows are the manure or waste flows; and the green arrows represent the production of pig feed. The percentages are the proportion of feed consumption in different pig rearing periods.

Weaned piglets (about 7.5 kg) are separated from the SOWS and start to be fed with solid feed. In the whole pig rearing stage, quality diets based on the nutritional requirements under different growth condi- tions are essential in maximizing the growth performance. First, these piglets are moved to a nursery facility and are housed with piglets from other litters, where they normally grow to 9-10 weeks of age (about 15-25 kg). After nursing, pigs enter the grower-finisher stage to gain weight until they are market-ready. Around 6-7 months of age, the market weight pigs (about 115 kg) are transported at a distance of approximately 370 km via truck for slaughter. A large amount of ex- creta is generated at the facility, separated into solid manure and slurry in house, and then further treated through compost and digestion.

We also include the upstream production of resources and energy that are used for pig production, e.g. tap water for drinking and washing the pig house, electricity for illumination, and sodium hydroxide for dis- infection. However, resource use and emissions related to infrastructure construction are not taken into account due to limited information. As recommended by the ISO standards, the system expansion method is used to deal with the multi-product systems. For instance, the fermented manure that can return to croplands as nutrient sources is as- sumed to avoid mineral fertilizer production (Laurent et al., 2014).

15: Developing a cradle-to-slaughterhouse gate LCI for small-scale in- tensive pig production systems requires both on-site investigations as well as the support from background LCI databases. In this study, pri- mary data about material and energy consumption, the composition of feed and the mixture with other ingredients at different pig produc- tion stages are derived from interviews with several pig production fa- cilities in Jiangsu Province, China. In addition, on-site emission factors like CH4, N20, NH3 and phosphorus are mainly collected from the existing literature. We also use the Ecoinvent database v3.5 for the up- stream production processes of materials and energy, including maize, soybean meal, wheat bran, salt, electricity and tap water. Note that pri- ority is given to those literature results or background LCIs that are suit- able for the current production situation in China. The data sources of each life cycle stage are presented in Table 1 and more details are illus- trated below.
Description of input and output data used in this study.

Data types|Sources|Related stages Cradle-to-gate LCI of raw materials (maize, wheat bran, barley, soybean meal, extruded soybean, calcium carbonate, salt)|Ecoinvent database v3.5|Feed production Feed composition and consumption Energy, water and material (sodium hydroxide) use|On-field investigation On-field investigation|Feed production Feed production Piglet production Pig rearing Slaughtering Transport distance|On-field investigation|Slaughtering Manure management Manure generation|On-field investigation (Guo and Wang, 2010)|Manure management N-related and C-related emission factors|(IPCC, 2019) (Yuan et al., 2018)|Piglet production Pig rearing Slaughtering Manure management
16: Developing a cradle-to-slaughterhouse gate LCI for small-scale in- tensive pig production systems requires both on-site investigations as well as the support from background LCI databases. In this study, pri- mary data about material and energy consumption, the composition of feed and the mixture with other ingredients at different pig produc- tion stages are derived from interviews with several pig production fa- cilities in Jiangsu Province, China. In addition, on-site emission factors like CH4, N20, NH3 and phosphorus are mainly collected from the existing literature. We also use the Ecoinvent database v3.5 for the up- stream production processes of materials and energy, including maize, soybean meal, wheat bran, salt, electricity and tap water. Note that pri- ority is given to those literature results or background LCIs that are suit- able for the current production situation in China. The data sources of each life cycle stage are presented in Table 1 and more details are illus- trated below.

17: Pig feed consists of three main energy and nutrition forms: carbohy- drates, proteins, and minerals. While crops like maize, wheat bran and barley provide a large amount of carbohydrates, soybean products, in- cluding extruded soybean and soybean meal, are richer in proteins. Minerals such as calcium carbonate are required as well to meet pig's calcium and other nutrient requirements. The most used and detected antibiotic in pig production, chlortetracycline (Lekagul et al., 2019; Zhou et al., 2020), is added into the material inputs and outputs, and linked with its upstream production process. Chinese regulation has allowed the use of chlortetracycline in pig rearing stage, with 0.2-0.3 g per kg of feed, but its use in piglet production stage is forbidden (MARA, 2017). The adsorption of antibiotics is poor in the animal gut, and approximately 30%-90% of antibiotics are excreted with manure (Kuppusamy et al., 2018). According to our field investigation, the pur- chased feed is crushed and mixed on the pig farm using different formu- las (Table 2) to support optimal and healthy growth in different stages. It is estimated that about 29.5 kWh of electricity is used to mix one ton of feed with a loss rate of 0.81%. Information about crop cultivation and pre-processing, e.g. granulation, is obtained from the Ecoinvent data- base. However, feed additives like vitamin supplements are not in- cluded in feed mainly because of the cut-off criteria (less than 5% in total) and data unavailability.

The main inputs for pig production include feed, electricity for light and water for cleaning and drinking, etc. The stage of pig rearing, whose primary mission is to gain weight, accounts for about 80% of the total feed consumption. In addition, the area of piggery is used to allocate the collected total consumption data for water and energy to different production stages. About 5.4 kg of manure (feces and urine) per pig is generated every day and temporarily stored beneath the slat- ted floor. Airborne emissions from the pig farm mainly include methane (CH4) from enteric fermentation. To calculate the CH4 emissions, we use the Tier 1 method from the most recent Intergovernmental Panel on Cli- mate Change (IPCC) guideline (IPCC, 2019), which requires information about the population size and the typical live weight (LW) of pig. Note that there are methodological updates in the IPCC guideline where CH4 emission coefficient is no longer differentiated between developed and developing countries, but by low and high productivity systems. Accordingly, the CH4 emission factor is assumed 1 kg head year-1. The input and output information for piglet production and pig rearing stages is described in Table S1.

22: The manure management system refers to the separate treatment of solid (70% of feces) and liquid manure (30% of feces are flushed away by urine). While solid manure is composted, liquid manure (slurry) flows out of the pig house to a biogas digester for digestion for 40 days, and then to an outdoor open slurry tank for storage for another 4 months. After that, the outputs of solid manure and digestates can be reused to nearby croplands as an effective source of nutrients. Airborne emissions from manure management mainly include CH4, CO2, N20, NH3, N2 and NO. The CH4 emissions generated during compost, anaerobic digestion, in-house and outdoor storage are calculated with the Tier 2 method in the new IPCC guideline. We first estimate the CH4 emission factor in each manure management stage by multiplying the maximum methane production capacity of pig manure and the corresponding methane con- version factor for each process (see Table S2). Then, the CH4 emissions are derived using the amount of volatile solids in either solid or liquid manure and the CH4 emission factors. Note that the volatile solids in liq- uid manure decrease by 60% during anaerobic digestion (Hou et al., 2017). The CO2 emission factor is in accordance with Guo et al.'s (2014) study. We estimate the N-related emissions during in-house and outdoor storage following a previous study (Yuan et al., 2018). The detail of emission factors is shown in Table S3. Due to the general mismanagement and the investment capacity of frontier techniques in small-scale pig farms (Meng, 2019), the leachate from compost and the emissions after slurry storage, including chemical oxygen demand, biochemical oxygen demand and phosphorus (P), is estimated accord- ing to a Chinese guideline (MEE, 2011). The output of fermented ma- nure is considered to substitute the possible manufacture of mineral fertilizers (Hanserud et al., 2018). Specifically, a credit for the substitu- tion of mineral fertilizer by applying the manure to nearby croplands is calculated on the basis of a mineral fertilizer equivalent of 75% for ni- trogen (N), 100% for P, 100% for potassium (K) (Brockmann et al., 2018; Vadenbo et al., 2017). Thus, 1 kg of N, P and K in the manure is assumed to substitute 0.75 kg, 1 kg and 1 kg of mineral N, P and K fertilizer, re- spectively. The upstream data of mineral fertilizer production is from the Ecoinvent database. Details of the inputs and outputs are presented in Table S4.
23: When growing to a typical live weight of around 115 kg, at about 6- 7 months of age, pigs are ready for slaughter and sale. First, pigs stay in the slaughterhouse for fasting for 24 h prior to slaughter, which reduces the stomach and intestinal contents and generates small amounts of manure, CH4 and NH3 in the abattoir. Then, pigs are slaughtered under conventional procedures that involve electrical stunning, bleeding, rins- ing, dehairing and evisceration, resulting in a carcass weight of around 83 kg. To produce 1000 kg of carcass weight, the slaughtering stage would consume 81 kWh of electricity and 4.2 tons of water. The estima- tion of CH4 and NH3 emissions are similar to that used in pig production. For hygienic reasons, the slaughterhouse uses a large amount of water in the slaughtering process and cleaning the facilities, which produces a large amount of wastewater. The wastewater, mainly containing di- luted blood, protein, fat, and suspended solids, is further treated in a sewage treatment plant (LCI is presented in Table S5).
24: Based on the LCI results, life cycle impact assessment (LCIA) is car- ried out by using the ReCiPe midpoint (H) 2016 method (Huijbregts et al., 2016), which is an updated version of ReCiPe 2008 and now pro- vides characterization factors that are representative with a global scope. Six impact categories are mostly used to evaluate the main life cycle environmental impact potentials, namely global warming (GWP), freshwater eutrophication (FEP), terrestrial acidification (TAP), freshwater ecotoxicity (FETP), human carcinogenic toxicity (HTP) and terrestrial ecotoxicity (TETP) (Cherubini et al., 2015; Noya
et al., 2017). Among them, GWP, FEP and TAP are the most concerned impact categories in previous studies (de Vries and de Boer, 2010; Reckmann et al., 2013).

The Monte Carlo (MC) simulation (1000 runs) is carried out in openLCA to examine how variation in the important parameters would affect the outcomes. A range in each key parameter is provided instead of using a single-point estimation. Two groups of parameters are included in this analysis: (i) chemical composition of manure, in- cluding volatile solids, total nitrogen, mineral nitrogen, and (ii) the C- related and N-related emission factors. The coefficients of variation (CVs) of parameters with little information are assumed using three cat- legories: high uncertainty (CV = 0.5), moderate uncertainty (CV = 0.25) and low uncertainty (CV = 0.1). The ranges for other parameters are based on relevant studies (IPCC, 2006; Zhu et al., 2015). Normal dis- tributions are applied in the majority of parameters and lognormal dis- tributions are applied in the N20 and N2 emission factors (Zhu et al., 2015). The 95% confidence interval is generated. The uncertainties of the key parameters are summarized in Table S6.

26: Table 3 summarizes the results of six environmental impact catego- ries due to the production of 1000 kg carcass weight in small-scale in- tensive pig production systems in China. In general, the majority of these impacts lie within or close to the ranges collected from previous studies except for the ecotoxicity indicators (Cherubini et al., 2015; Noya et al., 2017; Reckmann et al., 2016). The detail of previous studies is shown in Table S7. Our result of GWP is relatively high but consistent with previous estimates, which may be mainly due to the differences in feed conversion ratio (FCR) among regions. FCR is a valuable perfor- mance indicator to reflect how effective a feeding strategy can be. For example, while FCR values in Brazil and Germany are 2.5 and 2.9, re- spectively (Cherubini et al., 2015; Reckmann et al., 2013), FCR in this study is around 3. The practical application of FCR may be affected by several factors, such as genetic selection of pigs, diet formulas and use of antibiotics, farm facilities and management practices (Agostini et al., 2014; Losinger, 1998; Reyer et al., 2017). Generally, large-scale pig farms have lower FCR and would lead to a smaller GWP value (Wang et al., 2015), which reveals the importance of improving environmental performance through frontier management techniques. The IPCC re- finement of calculation of CH4 emissions in enteric fermentation and manure management may cause higher results of CH4 emissions. An- other reason could be the lacking of further utilization of biogas gener- ated by manure management in this study, leading to even higher emissions of CH4 (Cherubini et al., 2015). The result of TETP is two or- ders of magnitude larger than the evaluations in Spain, Cuba and Brazil, which may be caused by the update of characterization factors of atmospheric Cu emissions from transportation in the ReCiPe method (from 34 to 1.2x106 kg 1,4-DCB/kg). characterization factor of chlortetracycline in the ReCiPe midpoint (H) 2016 framework. Therefore, the ecotoxicity category does not cover the potential toxicity risk caused by antibiotics.

Feed production dominates the cradle-to-slaughterhouse gate envi- ronmental impacts in all impact categories (Fig. 2), accounting for over 56-95% of the total impact values. Specifically, feed required for 1 ton carcass weight of pig production in China generates 3351 kg CO2-eq, 25 kg SO2-eq, 1.5 kg P-eq, 105 kg 1,4-DCB of FETP, 7714 kg 1,4-DCB of TETP and 103 kg 1,4-DCB of HTP Among various feed ingredients, maize and soybean products show significant contributions, mainly due to the large consumption and the relatively intensive energy use. For example, a large amount of steam is used in the granulation process to cook and sterilize grains. Manure management is also a hotspot re- garding GWP, FEP and TAP impacts. More specifically (see Fig. S2), in comparison with CO2 emissions (36%) that are mostly generated from feed production, CH4 emissions in manure management contribute about 14% of the total GWP. As for FEP, phosphorus emissions from up- stream maize production take a leading part in causing freshwater eu- trophication problems (37%), probably attributable to the excess use of chemical fertilizers. Acidifying substances (e.g. NH3, SO2, and NOx) that lead to terrestrial acidification mainly come from manure manage- ment and upstream feed production, with NH3 emissions showing the primary contribution (60%), followed by SO2 (27%) and NO (13%). The avoided mineral fertilizer production reduces GWP, TAP and FEP for about 132 kg CO2-eq, 0.7 kg SO2-eq, 0.08 kg P-eq, respectively. The disparity in TAP results between studies may be driven by the use of dif- ferent pig operation facilities (Wang et al., 2017) and protein sources (Liu et al., 2017) that could lead to different NH3 emission factors. For example, the in-house NH3 emissions from the pull-plug system, which is quite common in Europe, are higher than other operation facil- ities (including this study) (Wang et al., 2017). The resulting toxic risks to ecosystems and human health are mainly caused by heavy metals (Cu, Zn, Cr, etc.) from upstream activities related to feed production, e.g. transportation, as well as pesticides (diflubenzuron) from upstream crop cultivation.
We find the uncertainties of C-related, N-related emission factors and the chemical composition of manure may have obvious impacts on GWP and TAP. Specifically, major uncertainties are observed for

GWP with a CV up to 10.1%. The impact of GWP ranges from 5568 kg CO2-eq to 7332 kg CO2-eq. The higher C-related and N20 emission fac- tors lead to a 48%, 48% and 79% increase of GWP in piglet production, pig rearing and manure management, respectively. Comparatively, TAP has a lower uncertainty and the CV is 9.8%, ranging from 31 kg SO2-eq to 44 kg SO2-eq. The uncertainty results of GWP and TAP are shown in Fig. S3.
27: Table 3 summarizes the results of six environmental impact catego- ries due to the production of 1000 kg carcass weight in small-scale in- tensive pig production systems in China. In general, the majority of these impacts lie within or close to the ranges collected from previous studies except for the ecotoxicity indicators (Cherubini et al., 2015; Noya et al., 2017; Reckmann et al., 2016). The detail of previous studies is shown in Table S7. Our result of GWP is relatively high but consistent with previous estimates, which may be mainly due to the differences in feed conversion ratio (FCR) among regions. FCR is a valuable perfor- mance indicator to reflect how effective a feeding strategy can be. For example, while FCR values in Brazil and Germany are 2.5 and 2.9, re- spectively (Cherubini et al., 2015; Reckmann et al., 2013), FCR in this study is around 3. The practical application of FCR may be affected by several factors, such as genetic selection of pigs, diet formulas and use of antibiotics, farm facilities and management practices (Agostini et al., 2014; Losinger, 1998; Reyer et al., 2017). Generally, large-scale pig farms have lower FCR and would lead to a smaller GWP value (Wang et al., 2015), which reveals the importance of improving environmental performance through frontier management techniques. The IPCC re- finement of calculation of CH4 emissions in enteric fermentation and manure management may cause higher results of CH4 emissions. An- other reason could be the lacking of further utilization of biogas gener- ated by manure management in this study, leading to even higher emissions of CH4 (Cherubini et al., 2015). The result of TETP is two or- ders of magnitude larger than the evaluations in Spain, Cuba and Brazil, which may be caused by the update of characterization factors of atmospheric Cu emissions from transportation in the ReCiPe method (from 34 to 1.2x106 kg 1,4-DCB/kg). Note that there is no ecotoxicity

31: Given the importance of pig feed, the comparative life cycle impacts of each feed formula are further evaluated (Fig. 3). It is highly associated with feed consumption in each growth phase. Among all feed formulas, environmental burden increases with the growth of pigs and is domi- nated by the final grower-finisher phase. The contribution of SOW and boar feed only represents 10-14% of the total impact values, quite sim- ilar to their contribution to total feed consumption. Note that boar feed in non-mating days is excluded from this life cycle.

While maize accounts for over a half (50-76%) of all the impacts of lactating sow, boar, nursery and 1st phase feed, its contribution de- creases in later growth stages due to the addition of other carbohydrate ingredients, e.g. wheat bran and barley. It is mainly driven by the lower costs of these ingredients due to the huge feed consumption in later growth stages. Compared to barley and maize, wheat bran has higher crude protein but its usage in feed formulas should be no more than 30%. The second largest contributor in most feed formulas is soybean meal, accounting for 11-38% in all impact categories, except for the ges- tating SOW feed. The consideration of feeding a high-carb diet to gestat- ing SOWS is to increase the ovulation rate as well as the litter size. Protein and energy requirements are greater in late gestation and lactation to prevent excessive weight loss for pregnant SOWS and those nursing the piglets. Protein accretion is also highly associated to lean gain. As pigs grow over 75 kg, the daily lean gain rates start to decline (Manu and Baidoo, 2020), indicating further lean gain at a much higher feed cost. Therefore, the proportion of soybean products in diet are reduced to optimize feed utilization. Barley is a key component to 2nd phase and 3rd phase feed, whose contribution range from 21-31% and 15-21% in most impact categories (Fig. S4). The results illustrate the

Fig. 2. Contribution of processes to each environmental impact category. The stacked bar chart shows the relevant contribution of stages to each environmental impact category, while the pie chart shows the main hotspots for feed production. The label of "Others" includes calcium carbonate, salt and electricity.
Fig. 3. Life cycle assessment for the different feed diets (per FU).
importance of optimizing the feed formulas in different phases of pig and improving feeding technologies for proper feed consumption.

Among all feed formulas, environmental burden increases with the growth of pigs and is domi- nated by the final grower-finisher phase. The contribution of SOW and boar feed only represents 10-14% of the total impact values, quite sim- ilar to their contribution to total feed consumption. Note that boar feed in non-mating days is excluded from this life cycle.

While maize accounts for over a half (50-76%) of all the impacts of lactating sow, boar, nursery and 1st phase feed, its contribution de- creases in later growth stages due to the addition of other carbohydrate ingredients, e.g. wheat bran and barley. It is mainly driven by the lower costs of these ingredients due to the huge feed consumption in later growth stages. Compared to barley and maize, wheat bran has higher crude protein but its usage in feed formulas should be no more than 30%. The second largest contributor in most feed formulas is soybean meal, accounting for 11-38% in all impact categories, except for the ges- tating SOW feed. The consideration of feeding a high-carb diet to gestat- ing SOWS is to increase the ovulation rate as well as the litter size. Protein and energy requirements are greater in late gestation and lactation to prevent excessive weight loss for pregnant SOWS and those nursing the piglets. Protein accretion is also highly associated to lean gain. As pigs grow over 75 kg, the daily lean gain rates start to decline (Manu and Baidoo, 2020), indicating further lean gain at a much higher feed cost. Therefore, the proportion of soybean products in diet are reduced to optimize feed utilization. Barley is a key component to 2nd phase and 3rd phase feed, whose contribution range from 21-31% and 15-21% in most impact categories (Fig. S4). The results illustrate the

Fig. 2. Contribution of processes to each environmental impact category. The stacked bar chart shows the relevant contribution of stages to each environmental impact category, while the pie chart shows the main hotspots for feed production. The label of "Others" includes calcium carbonate, salt and electricity.

importance of optimizing the feed formulas in different phases of pig and improving feeding technologies for proper feed consumption.
35: Considering the complete life cycle of pigs, feed maintains the most important material input in terms of all the impact categories. Research is underway to improve feed utilization towards more sustainable pig production. While maize is the most common energy ingredient used in China, pig feed in European countries is richer in wheat. It has been demonstrated that wheat-based diet has better environmental perfor- mance than maize-based diet (van der Werf et al., 2005), but this may trigger food security worries since wheat is a major staple food in China. As for protein-rich ingredients, feed-use amino acids can be used to replace soybean meal in the conventional pig feed without changing the existing nutrient provision (Garcia-Launay et al., 2014; Monteiro et al., 2016). Other non-human-edible feedstuffs are also rich in essential nutrients for pig feed formulation and would lead to lower environmental impacts. For example, the inclusion of macaúba cake, a co-product derived from native palm trees in Latin America, has the potential to reduce dietary GWP by 3.4-7.0% (Ali et al., 2017; Mackenzie et al., 2016). Adding phytase can improve the digestibility of P and thus reduce P excretion (Harper et al., 1997).

Feed conversion ratio is another significant parameter that affects environmental performance, which is influenced by a combination of factors including not only feed composition, but also operation facilities and management practices. For example, better FCR is achieved if pigs are placed in pens with less than 50% slatted floors, since less feed would be lost through the slats (Agostini et al., 2014). New feeding tech- nologies like sensor-controlled smart feeding systems for precision management are currently commercially available (Monteiro et al., 2016). The influence of population density on FCR is evident as well. By controlling the batches with fewer than 20 animals per pen, an im- provement in FCR is observed (Pierozan et al., 2016).

Beyond feed production, the importance of manure management to cumulative environmental burdens needs further exploration. The cur- rent in-house slurry system and outdoor open-air tank are a quite com- mon and relatively cheap combination to treat manure. However, there are concerns regarding both odor and greenhouse gases for this option (Tilman et al., 2002). Previous studies have found that biogas from pig manure can be captured and further used to produce heat and electric- ity, turning the greenhouse gas emissions into a climate benefit (Cherubini et al., 2015; Pexas et al., 2020; Yuan et al., 2018). Addition- ally, slurry acidification techniques that lower the pH of pig manure can prevent ammonia from evaporating and thus reduce airborne acid- ification and eutrophication (Pexas et al., 2020).

38: Our results of the most concerned impact categories are generally higher than those in a study of a large-scale pig farm (Wang et al., 2015). By comparing the provided inventories, the consumption of feed, especially maize, in the small-scale pig farm is higher than the large-scale one. The poorer FCR in the small-scale pig farm may be the driver for worse performance. Although the large-scale pig farm shows better environmental performance, small-scale systems would still be a major constituent of China's pig production farms in the short future. This emphasizes the importance of comprehensively un- derstanding the environmental burdens of small-scale pig farms to pro- vide environmental mitigation strategies for policy-makers.

Although pork accounts for a large portion of meat consumption in China, the consumption of beef and poultry is also increasing, as well as aquatic products (He et al., 2016; Xiong et al., 2020). To consider the comparative cradle-to-slaughterhouse gate impacts of different kinds of meat foods (Arrieta and Gonzalez, 2019; Asem-Hiablie et al., 2019; Ray et al., 2019), we normalize all the functional units to equal protein intake according to FAO suggestions (11.2, 16.5 and 22.8 g pro- tein per 100 of pork, beef and chicken, respectively). The results show considerably larger environmental impacts of beef than pork and chicken (Fig. 4a, b), mainly driven by higher feed conversion ratios, lon- ger housing time, and the enteric fermentation from ruminating ani- mals (Presumido et al., 2018; Tichenor et al., 2017). Developing countries, in general, perform rather poorly in terms of greenhouse gas emissions from pig production (Fig. 4c), which indicates the possi- bility of further reductions in GWP by drawing on the experience of de- veloped countries (Luna-Nemecio et al., 2020). Aquatic products are more GWP-efficient than terrestrial livestock products, e.g. 12.25 kg CO2-eq per kg protein from salmon and 15.72 kg CO2-eq per kg protein from oyster, respectively (Ray et al., 2019; Sherry and Koester, 2020). But there is growing concern about nutrient release in pond effluents (Wang et al., 2020) or accumulation in pond sediments (Pouil et al., 2019) that could lead to eutrophication. Additionally, some future foods, such as cultured meat, fly larvae and algae, are also promising candidates for sustainable protein sources for humans (Parodi et al., 2018).

40: It is important to acknowledge that this analysis is conducted con- sidering a stable pig production state. However, some unexpected shocks, such as extreme weather events and geopolitical crises, may break this stability of the supply chain (Cottrell et al., 2019). Since 2018, the epidemic of African swine fever has reduced 480 thousand tons of pig production in China. Farmers have to burn the pig carcasses that died of the disease and use extra disinfectants to prevent further spreading. Moreover, because of the recent COVID-19 pandemic, food supply chain was once cut off from both the supply and demand side (Hobbs, 2020). Because of the closures or slowdowns at meat process- ing plants in the U.S., pigs have to stay in the house or be euthanized even they are marker-ready, which changes the cumulative

Fig. 4. Environmental impacts of competing meat sources in different regions. (a) and (b) the GWP and TAP of competing meat sources in different regions. (c) the per capita income $ with the GWP outcomes in different regions. [Data are from Total Economy Database of the Groningen Growth and Development Centre, 2020].
environmental performance. Future studies could be performed to as- sess the change of environmental impacts due to production shocks.

Several other assumptions are made to construct the life cycle inven- tory of small-intensive intensive pig production systems in China, which may also have influences on the environmental outcomes. For example, soybean is one of the main ingredients for pig feed in China, of which 74% are imported from South America in 2019, especially Brazil (MARA, 2020). We roughly estimate the inter-continental transport dis- tance of soybeans using the SEA-DISTANCES calculator (23,922 km), as- suming this inclusion would have limited influence on the overall results (Reckmann et al., 2013; Winkler et al., 2016). In addition, mate- rials used in pig house, e.g. veterinary medicinal products, are not taken into account based on the cut-off criteria (less than 5% of the total inputs).

Several other assumptions are made to construct the life cycle inven- tory of small-intensive intensive pig production systems in China, which may also have influences on the environmental outcomes. For example, soybean is one of the main ingredients for pig feed in China, of which 74% are imported from South America in 2019, especially Brazil (MARA, 2020). We roughly estimate the inter-continental transport dis- tance of soybeans using the SEA-DISTANCES calculator (23,922 km), as- suming this inclusion would have limited influence on the overall results (Reckmann et al., 2013; Winkler et al., 2016). In addition, mate- rials used in pig house, e.g. veterinary medicinal products, are not taken into account based on the cut-off criteria (less than 5% of the total inputs).
43: The Chinese system for small-scale intensive pig production has been analyzed from the environmental perspective. Our results are con- sistent with previous research, revealing the significant predominant influence (59-95%) of feed production on overall impacts. Maize, followed by soybean products, is the major contributor to all associated environmental outcomes. By comparing with other operation systems across the world, we find developing countries (including China), in general, perform rather poorly in terms of greenhouse gas emissions. Changes in feed consumption and composition along the lifetime

growth indicate a much higher contribution from the grower-finisher stage. Our research suggests reducing the impacts associated with small-intensive pig production systems by optimizing feed formulas, in- troducing new feed technologies, and upgrading manure management systems. The results together with the potential further environmental improvements serve as an effective tool for small-scale producers in China to move towards more sustainable pig production.
